techniques.evidence_quote: a hacker breached an employee account, got access to Drizly's corporate GitHub login information
Inactive GitHub account compromised via reused password
Drizly stored login credentials on GitHub contrary to the platform's own guidance
Inactive GitHub account compromised via reused password
hacked into the company's database, and then stole customers' information
got access to Drizly's corporate GitHub login information, hacked into the company's database
hacked into the company's database, and then stole customers' information
hackers were able to use Drizly's servers to mine cryptocurrency until the company changed its login information for its cloud computing account

attack_chain.description: Attacker compromised an inactive Drizly employee GitHub account by exploiting a reused password, likely sourced from a prior credential breach.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Inactive GitHub account compromised via reused password

attack_chain.description: Using the compromised GitHub account, the attacker discovered AWS cloud computing login credentials (and/or corporate GitHub credentials) stored in plaintext within the GitHub repository — a practice Drizly had been warned about since a 2018 incident where an employee posted cloud account login info on GitHub.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Drizly stored login credentials on GitHub contrary to the platform's own guidance and well-publicized security incidents involving GitHub

attack_chain.description: The attacker used the harvested AWS/corporate credentials to authenticate to Drizly's cloud environment hosted on Amazon Web Services.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a hacker breached an employee account, got access to Drizly's corporate GitHub login information, hacked into the company's database

attack_chain.description: In the 2018 precursor incident (after an employee posted cloud login info on GitHub), hackers used Drizly's AWS servers to mine cryptocurrency before the company rotated its credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: hackers were able to use Drizly's servers to mine cryptocurrency until the company changed its login information for its cloud computing account

attack_chain.description: In the 2020 breach, after gaining access via valid cloud credentials, the attacker identified and located Drizly's customer database (RDS instance) within the AWS environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: hacked into the company's database, and then stole customers' information

attack_chain.description: The attacker exfiltrated the personal information of approximately 2.5 million consumers from Drizly's AWS-hosted database, including email addresses, postal addresses, phone numbers, device identifiers, and geolocation data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: hacked into the company's database, and then stole customers' information

initial_access_vector: Inactive employee GitHub account compromised via reused/credential-stuffed password, which exposed AWS cloud credentials stored in the GitHub repository
persistence_mechanism: Use of valid cloud account credentials obtained from GitHub repository; no additional persistence mechanism described in the source
privilege_escalation: Not described in the source; attacker appears to have had sufficient access via the discovered credentials without requiring further escalation
